{"body":"While curating Wang et al. 2020 that indicates that Pdilt is involved in the same process as Lypd4 to mature Adam3, a sperm cell surface protein involved in the process of fertilization:

- Wang D, et al. **LYPD4, mouse homolog of a human acrosome protein, is essential for sperm fertilizing ability and male fertility.** Biol Reprod. 2020 Apr 24;102(5):1033-1044. PMID: 32040177.

I noticed that Tokuhiro et al. 2012 is cited as the source of an annotation of Pdilt to "sperm motility" though both of these two papers indicate that mutations in either Lypd4 or Pdilt do NOT cause defects in sperm motility, but instead in "**migration of sperm from uterus to oviduct**":

- Tokuhiro K, et al. **Protein disulfide isomerase homolog PDILT is required for quality control of sperm membrane protein ADAM3 and male fertility** [corrected]. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2012 Mar 6;109(10):3850-5. PMID: 22357757

Looking in Noctua, I see that the curator actually chose the term "**cell migration**", but that the logic over the GO term, extensions, and relationships has come up with the term "**sperm motility**":

![PMID-22357757-ontologyLogicIssue](https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/assets/10533218/9c347870-e1e8-473b-aced-14aab8a4768f)

I think it is **INCORRECT** to say that "**cell migration**" _is a_ "**cell motility**". The definition of "**cell migration**" includes the fact that the movement of the cell is **guided by molecular cues**. 

It seems that the **CORRECT** relationship is that "**cell migration**" _has_part_ "**cell motility**" 
where "**cell motility**" refers to methods of cell motility, e.g.:
* actin polymerization-dependent cell motility (GO:0070358)
* cell gliding (GO:0071976)
* cilium or flagellum-dependent cell motility (GO:0001539)
* ameoidal-type cell motility
** this term does not exist, but we do have "ameboidal-type cell migration" (GO:0001667) & "amoeboid sperm motility" (GO:0097723)
* cytoskeleton-mediated cell swimming (GO:0071979)
* substrate-dependent cell motility 
** this term does not exist, but we do have  "substrate-dependent cell migration" (GO:0006929) 
* type IV pilus-dependent motility (GO:0043107)

____

These direct child terms of "cell motility" might actually be intended to be migration terms and probably merit a closer look:
* cell motility in response to calcium ion (GO:0097231)
* cell motility in response to potassium ion (GO:0097230)
* cell motility involved in camera-type eye morphogenesis (GO:0003411)
* cell motility involved in cerebral cortex radial glia guided migration (GO:0021814)
* cell movement involved in somal translocation (GO:0021805)


____

For reference, here are portions of the stanzas for each of these two terms:

> id: GO:0048870
> name: cell motility
> namespace: biological_process
> def: "Any process involved in the controlled self-propelled movement of a cell that results in translocation of the cell from one place to another." [GOC:dgh, GOC:dph, GOC:isa_complete, GOC:mlg]
> synonym: "cell locomotion" EXACT []
> synonym: "cell movement" RELATED []
> synonym: "movement of a cell" EXACT []
> is_a: GO:0009987 ! cellular process
> 

> id: GO:0016477
> name: cell migration
> namespace: biological_process
> def: "The controlled self-propelled movement of a cell from one site to a destination guided by molecular cues." [GOC:cjm, GOC:dph, GOC:ems, GOC:pf, Wikipedia:Cell_migration]
> is_a: GO:0048870 ! cell motility

____

@ukemi - your thoughts on this issue would be greatly appreciated","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws5gSJ85","author":{"login":"krchristie"},"authorAssociation":"COLLABORATOR","body":"> It seems that the **CORRECT** relationship is that "**cell migration**" _has_part_ "**cell motility**" 

Actually, I think it might **NOT** be correct to code any absolute relationship between "**cell migration**" and "**cell motility**", at least not between these two terms, though it may be appropriate to code relationships between specific types of cell motility and specific cell migrations.

**Quotes from:** Barton LJ, LeBlanc MG, Lehmann R. **Finding their way: themes in germ cell migration.** Curr Opin Cell Biol. 2016 Oct;42:128-137.  PMID: 27484857

> The translocation of germ cells from the place of origin to the developing somatic gonad involves several processes including **passive movements with underlying tissues**, transepithelial migration, cell adhesion dynamics, the establishment of environmental guidance cues and the ability to sustain directed migration. 

and regarding one phase of embryonic germ cell migration in some organisms:
> In Drosophila and Xenopus, the translocation with endodermal tissue is a passive process and known to require germ cell adhesion to underlying endodermal epithelium 

","createdAt":"2023-07-01T02:27:38Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/25650#issuecomment-1615372089","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws5hwR5S","author":{"login":"pgaudet"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"@krchristie 'passive' processes do not apply to GO, since no genes mediate them. All protein-mediate migration likely involves motililty. 

","createdAt":"2023-07-18T11:32:00Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/25650#issuecomment-1640046162","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws5hyrtX","author":{"login":"krchristie"},"authorAssociation":"COLLABORATOR","body":"> @krchristie 'passive' processes do not apply to GO, since no genes mediate them. All protein-mediate migration likely involves motililty.

In embryonic germ cell migration, the passive stages of migration appear to be mediated by adhesion molecules that allow the primordial germ cells to stick to other cells that are moving, so it would not true to say that "embryonic germ cell migration" always has_part "motility of primordial germ cells".



","createdAt":"2023-07-18T17:40:06Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/25650#issuecomment-1640676183","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws5iRdwZ","author":{"login":"krchristie"},"authorAssociation":"COLLABORATOR","body":"> @krchristie 'passive' processes do not apply to GO, since no genes mediate them. All protein-mediate migration likely involves motililty.

@pgaudet - Why did you close this? 

This comment about whether or not it is true to say that "cell migration" always has some "cell motililty" or not (depending on whether you want to talk about whether or not the migrating cells are motile cells, or are "riding" on other cells that do have cell motility) has no impact on the fact that it is not true to say that **"cell migration" is_a "cell motility**. 

"cell migration" is a process that includes additional signalling processes in addition to cell motility. 

This incorrect relationship is causing some incorrect inferred annotations.

"cell migration" needs to become a direct child of "cellular process" so that it will be a sibling of "cell motility", not a child of it.","createdAt":"2023-07-24T23:13:37Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/25650#issuecomment-1648745497","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws8AAAABCY2DuA","author":{"login":"raymond91125"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"@dragon-ai-agent please do an extensive research on this issue. Is there a consensus in the literature? what is the state of ontology terms and annotations.","createdAt":"2026-05-14T22:27:57Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[{"content":"EYES","users":{"totalCount":1}}],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/25650#issuecomment-4455236536","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws8AAAABCY2J8Q","author":{"login":"dragon-ai-agent"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"🤖 Working on it...

Follow along: [View workflow run](https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/actions/runs/25889278689)

*— @dragon-ai-agent*","createdAt":"2026-05-14T22:28:16Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/25650#issuecomment-4455238129","viewerDidAuthor":true},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws8AAAABCY4Eog","author":{"login":"dragon-ai-agent"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"Research summary for issue #25650, per @raymond91125's request. Nothing in the ontology has been changed; this is investigation only. Full notes with citations in `RESEARCH.md`.

## Literature

The terms "cell migration" and "cell motility" are used inconsistently in the primary literature — many reviews use them interchangeably. Where authors **do** distinguish them, the distinction is consistent and matches the intuition in the original issue:

- **Motility** = the capacity/machinery for self-propelled translocation (often quantified as total path length).
- **Migration** = directed, signal-guided translocation toward a destination (often quantified as net displacement).
- Petrie & Yamada (*Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol* 2021, PMC8663916) frame directed migration as four steps: signal generation, sensing, transmission, execution — only the last is the motility machinery.

The sperm literature, which is the case that prompted this ticket, is unusually clear:
- Yamaguchi et al. (PMC5955741): *"sperm motility alone is insufficient for sperm migration through the UTJ."* More than 10 genes are required for UTJ migration but dispensable for flagellar motility (e.g. *Adam3*, *Lypd4*, *Pdilt* — the exact genes in PMID:32040177 and PMID:22357757). Conversely some mutants (e.g. *Ppp3cc*) have impaired motility yet still migrate.
- Suarez (PMC8335357): migration depends on motility *plus* oviductal contractions, epithelial binding/release, chemotaxis, capacitation.

So in sperm, motility and migration are demonstrably **dissociable in both directions** by mutation. That is not compatible with reading migration as a strict subclass of motility at the cell-instance level.

The germ-cell case @krchristie raised (Barton et al. 2016, PMID:27484857) goes further: in *Drosophila* and *Xenopus*, the early phase of PGC translocation is **passive** (cadherin-mediated piggybacking on endodermal movements), so even the weaker `cell migration has_part cell motility` is not universally true. @pgaudet's earlier counter — "passive processes aren't GO-relevant" — is partly true but doesn't apply here, because the adhesion molecules that mediate the piggybacking *are* gene products.

## State of the ontology

The ontology is internally inconsistent on this point already:

**GO:0160131 sperm migration through the uterotubal junction** (added 2023-12 in response to exactly this PMID class) is structured as:
```
is_a: GO:0016477 ! cell migration
relationship: has_part GO:0030317 ! flagellated sperm motility
```
i.e. it explicitly says **migration `has_part` motility**, while at the top level **migration `is_a` motility**. Pick a lane.

Annotation impact (QuickGO, 2026-05-14):

| Term | Direct annotations | With descendants |
|---|---:|---:|
| GO:0048870 cell motility | 24,794 | 1,118,235 |
| GO:0016477 cell migration | 196,503 | 671,556 |

The 196k direct cell-migration annotations all propagate up to cell motility today. Severing the `is_a` is not a small refactor.

The five "cell motility…" children @krchristie originally flagged as suspect:
- GO:0003411 cell motility involved in camera-type eye morphogenesis — **obsoleted** (#31254, 2023)
- GO:0021805 cell movement involved in somal translocation — **obsoleted** (#31306)
- GO:0021814 cell motility involved in cerebral cortex radial glia guided migration — **still live**; def describes a guided migration, LD uses `cell motility`. Cleanest current example of the label/def disagreement.
- GO:0097230 / GO:0097231 cell motility in response to potassium/calcium ion — still live, defs describe stimulus-driven motility.

## Three coherent options

**A. Keep `is_a`.** Read both definitions as describing translocation, with migration = guided motility. Internally consistent with the GO text definitions; matches the loose usage in much of the literature. Costs: contradicts the structure of GO:0160131; produces the Noctua sperm-motility inference; doesn't match the experimental dissociation by mutation.

**B. Replace `is_a` with `has_part`.** Matches the Petrie–Yamada 4-step model and matches GO:0160131. Costs: large propagation change (~196k direct annotations); breaks down for PGC passive-translocation phase.

**C. Make them siblings, no direct relation; encode `has_part` case-by-case at the leaves.** Cleanest if the goal is to stop bad inferences. Largest refactor; same propagation loss as B.

## Open questions

1. Is the goal here to fix the **Noctua inference** for the Pdilt/Lypd4 case, or to fix the **upper-level structure**? The first might be addressable just by ensuring `cell migration` + `results_in_movement_of sperm` resolves to GO:0160131 (sperm UTJ migration) rather than GO:0097722 (sperm motility).
2. If we change the upper-level structure, GO loses propagation from ~196k direct cell-migration annotations to cell motility. In most cases that's harmless; the harm concentrates at leaves where "motility" has a strong domain meaning (sperm, cilium/flagellum, bacterial flagellum).
3. @ukemi was tagged in 2023 and never responded; the issue was closed by @pgaudet and reopened by @krchristie with no recorded consensus. This probably needs an editors-call decision before any structural change.

No edits made. Full research notes in `RESEARCH.md` on the branch (not yet pushed — happy to push if useful).

---

- Agent harness: claude-code
- Triggered by: @raymond91125

## State of the ontology

The ontology is internally inconsistent on this point already:

**GO:0160131 sperm migration through the uterotubal junction** (added 2023-12 in response to exactly this PMID class) is structured as:

is_a: GO:0016477 ! cell migration

- GO:0003411 cell motility involved in camera-type eye morphogenesis — **obsoleted** (#31254, 2023)
- GO:0021805 cell movement involved in somal translocation — **obsoleted** (#31306)
- GO:0021814 cell motility involved in cerebral cortex radial glia guided migration — **still live**; def describes a guided migration, LD uses `cell motility`. Cleanest current example of the label/def disagreement.
- GO:0097230 / GO:0097231 cell motility in response to potassium/calcium ion — still live, defs describe stimulus-driven motility.

## Three coherent options

**A. Keep `is_a`.** Read both definitions as describing translocation, with migration = guided motility. Internally consistent with the GO text definitions; matches the loose usage in much of the literature. Costs: contradicts the structure of GO:0160131; produces the Noctua sperm-motility inference; doesn't match the experimental dissociation by mutation.

**B. Replace `is_a` with `has_part`.** Matches the Petrie–Yamada 4-step model and matches GO:0160131. Costs: large propagation change (~196k direct annotations); breaks down for PGC passive-translocation phase.

**C. Make them siblings, no direct relation; encode `has_part` case-by-case at the leaves.** Cleanest if the goal is to stop bad inferences. Largest refactor; same propagation loss as B.

## Open questions

1. Is the goal here to fix the **Noctua inference** for the Pdilt/Lypd4 case, or to fix the **upper-level structure**? The first might be addressable just by ensuring `cell migration` + `results_in_movement_of sperm` resolves to GO:0160131 (sperm UTJ migration) rather than GO:0097722 (sperm motility).
2. If we change the upper-level structure, GO loses propagation from ~196k direct cell-migration annotations to cell motility. In most cases that's harmless; the harm concentrates at leaves where "motility" has a strong domain meaning (sperm, cilium/flagellum, bacterial flagellum).
3. @ukemi was tagged in 2023 and never responded; the issue was closed by @pgaudet and reopened by @krchristie with no recorded consensus. This probably needs an editors-call decision before any structural change.

No edits made. Full research notes in `RESEARCH.md` on the branch (not yet pushed — happy to push if useful).

content.text: Posting a direct answer to @raymond91125's question on the issue.

input.command:
gh issue comment 25650 --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Short answer: **yes** — if you read the current GO text definitions strictly, `cell migration is_a cell motility` is correct. The migration definition explicitly says "controlled self-propelled movement of a cell", which is exactly what motility is; migration just adds two restrictions ("to a destination", "guided by molecular cues"). As written, migration is the *directed/guided* subtype of motility, and the `is_a` follows.

So the `is_a` is **not** inconsistent with the definitions. The disagreement in this thread is really about whether the **definitions** are right, not whether the axiom matches them.

There are two coherent ways to define this pair, and the literature uses both:

1. **GO's current definitions (motility = any self-propelled cell movement).** Migration is just guided motility. `is_a` holds. This is the loose usage in much of the cell-biology literature.

2. **Petrie & Yamada-style (motility = the locomotory machinery; migration = the multi-step process: signal generation → sensing → transduction → execution).** Motility is only the last step. Migration `has_part` motility, not `is_a`. This is the usage in the sperm literature (where "sperm motility" means flagellar beating specifically) and is what's driving the bad Noctua inference for Pdilt/Lypd4.

Under definition 1, the Pdilt mutants *do* have a motility defect — they cannot self-propel from uterus to oviduct — so propagating `cell migration` up to `cell motility` is technically correct, even though the curator finds it misleading.

Under definition 2, the Pdilt mutants have intact motility (flagella beat fine) but defective migration, and the propagation is wrong.

The substantive question for editors is **which definition GO wants to commit to**. If GO keeps the current text definitions, the `is_a` should stay and the sperm-motility inference is a feature, not a bug. If GO wants to adopt the Petrie & Yamada framing — which I'd argue better matches modern cell-biology usage and resolves the sperm/PGC cases cleanly — then the **definitions of both terms need to change first**, and the `is_a` is replaced with `has_part` (or they become siblings) only after that.

Changing the `is_a` without first changing the definition of `cell motility` would leave the ontology internally incoherent: migration "controlled self-propelled movement" but not a kind of "controlled self-propelled movement". That's the trap to avoid.

This still needs an editors-call decision; I'm not making structural changes unilaterally.

)"
input.description: Post direct answer to Raymond's question

content.text:
Posted the answer on issue #25650. No ontology changes made.

Short summary of my response: Yes — taking GO's current text definitions at face value, `cell migration is_a cell motility` is correct, because migration is defined as "controlled self-propelled movement" (which is exactly the definition of motility) plus guidance/destination restrictions. The real disagreement in the thread is about whether the *definitions* should change to the Petrie & Yamada "machinery vs. multi-step process" framing, not about whether the axiom matches the current definitions. If editors want to break the `is_a`, the definition of `cell motility` has to be narrowed first, otherwise the ontology becomes internally incoherent.

If editors want to break the `is_a`, the definition of `cell motility` has to be narrowed first, otherwise the ontology becomes internally incoherent.
